notes: Primary opcodes 0x00-0x5E are ordinary base instructions.
Primary opcode 0x5F is an extension prefix; the following byte selects the extended opcode.
Primary opcodes 0x60-0xFF are packed one-byte families with embedded fields and no inline operands.
Jump-family opcodes 0x30-0x3E share the same mixed target encoding: a nonzero byte is a signed relative displacement, while a zero byte selects a following absolute little-endian target word.
SETJMP carries two packed code-location words whose high bytes select 256-byte blocks and whose low bytes select intra-block offsets.

pop_count and push_count record the operand-stack effect recovered from current Linchpin implementations; null means the count varies by encoding or runtime path, or that current Linchpin code does not provide a reliable fixed stack effect for that opcode.

operand_kinds.description: Unsigned byte immediate naming a byte index within a vector or tuple. Byte index 0 begins immediately after visible word slot 0.
Unsigned byte immediate naming a visible word slot within a vector or tuple.
Unsigned byte immediate naming the number of explicit call arguments or returned words, depending on opcode family.
Little-endian control word consumed by the bitfield helper families.
Unsigned byte selecting an XDISP suboperation.
Unsigned byte selecting a DISP suboperation.
Little-endian packed far-call selector. The high byte is the 1-based module number and the low byte is the procedure-table index within that module.
Little-endian unsigned word embedded directly in the instruction stream.
Unsigned byte embedded directly in the instruction stream and widened to a VM word when pushed.
Mixed branch encoding. The first operand byte is a signed relative displacement when nonzero; when it is zero, the next two bytes are an absolute little-endian target word.
Unsigned byte naming a local variable slot.
Unsigned byte naming a module-global slot. Some out-of-range values in the 0xC0-0xDB band map to interpreter-owned state.
Little-endian module-relative code offset used by fixed-arity near calls.
Implicit padding consumed by NEXTB. Execution resumes at the next 256-byte code-block boundary after skipping any remaining bytes in the current block.
Unsigned byte selecting an OPEN mode. The full mode table is still incomplete, but the operand encoding is fixed.
Little-endian packed code location. The high byte selects a 256-byte block relative to the active module and the low byte selects the offset within that block.
Unsigned byte naming a program-global slot.
Unsigned byte giving the number of words returned by RETN.
Unsigned byte giving the number of stack words discarded by a counted pop operation.
Little-endian status or halt code embedded directly in the stream.
